title: Ephesians
author: Paul, the apostle, from prison (~AD 60–62)
date: ~AD 60–62, one of the 'prison epistles' (with Philippians, Colossians, Philemon)
setting: Possibly a circular letter to several churches in Asia Minor (some manuscripts omit 'in Ephesus' in 1:1). Ephesus was Paul's longest ministry base (Acts 19); the letter assumes readers already know the gospel and writes to build their theological understanding and community life.
purpose: To unfold the cosmic scope of God's redemptive plan in Christ — blessing the church with every spiritual blessing, uniting Jew and Gentile into one new humanity, and calling the church to live worthy of its calling in every sphere of life.
themes: The church as the body of Christ — one new humanity
The household code: wives/husbands, children/fathers, servants/masters

outline.label: Doxology: Every Spiritual Blessing in Christ
Salvation by Grace, Dead Made Alive
Jew and Gentile: One New Humanity

before.label: Paul's long ministry in Ephesus — three years (Acts 19)
Paul's arrest in Jerusalem; two years in Caesarean custody
Colossians — the cosmic supremacy of Christ established

after.label: Philippians — joy from prison; the mind of Christ
Revelation — the church as Christ's bride, the new Jerusalem

key_people.name: Paul
key_people.role: Author (the letter claims Pauline authorship; if pseudonymous, it represents a Pauline school's mature reflection on his theology) writing from prison with the broadest possible horizon for the church's identity

key_people.name: Tychicus
key_people.role: The letter's carrier (6:21-22) who will supplement its contents in person — a reminder that Pauline letters were oral events, read aloud and explained by trusted emissaries

context: Ephesians was written c. AD 60-62 from Rome (if authentic Pauline), possibly as a circular letter to several Asian churches rather than specifically to Ephesus (the earliest manuscripts omit 'in Ephesus' in 1:1). The letter lacks the personal greetings and specific controversy that characterise most Pauline letters, suggesting a more general audience. It represents Paul's most cosmic and ecclesiological vision, addressed to churches that need to understand their place in God's eternal purposes.
christ_connection: Ephesians presents the most comprehensive NT picture of the cosmic scope of Christ's work. He has been raised above every ruler, authority, power, and dominion (1:21) and given as head over all things to the church (1:22); he has broken down the wall between Jew and Gentile, creating one new humanity in himself (2:14-15); he fills everything in every way (1:23; 4:10). The church, as his body and fullness, is the visible expression of his cosmic rule — the place where the multiform wisdom of God is made known to the powers and authorities (3:10). Marriage is a mystery that images Christ and the church (5:32).

key_verses.note: 'He chose us in him before the foundation of the world, that we should be holy and blameless before him' — election in Christ as the foundation of the entire letter's theology of grace

'For by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God, not a result of works, so that no one may boast' — the most concise statement of salvation by grace through faith in the NT
'Through the church the manifold wisdom of God might now be made known to the rulers and authorities in the heavenly places' — the church's cosmic purpose: to display God's wisdom to spiritual powers
The sevenfold unity: one body, one Spirit, one hope, one Lord, one faith, one baptism, one God and Father — the ontological basis for the practical unity the letter calls for

themes_detail.title: Election, Adoption, and the Eternal Purpose of God
themes_detail.text: Ephesians 1:3-14 is one long, breathless sentence in Greek (a single period in the Greek text) cataloguing the spiritual blessings that belong to those who are in Christ. Election before the foundation of the world (1:4), predestination to adoption (1:5), redemption through his blood (1:7), and the sealing of the Spirit (1:13-14) are all anchored in God's sovereign, prior choice. Paul's purpose in this opening is doxological: the goal of these purposes is 'the praise of his glorious grace' (1:6, 12, 14). The entire letter stands under the sign of grace — undeserved, unconditional, sovereign.

themes_detail.title: The Church as the New Humanity
themes_detail.text: Ephesians 2:11-22 presents the church as the most radical social reality in the world: Jew and Gentile, formerly separated by the law's 'dividing wall of hostility' (2:14), have been made one new humanity in Christ's body through the cross. This is not merely ethnic reconciliation but a new creation — a community that transcends the deepest division in the ancient world. The church's unity across ethnic, social, and cultural lines is therefore not a secondary sociological concern but the very body of Christ, the visible expression of his cosmic reconciling work. Failure to maintain this unity contradicts the gospel.

themes_detail.title: The Cosmic Lordship of Christ and the Powers
themes_detail.text: Ephesians develops a distinctive theology of 'principalities and powers' — spiritual beings that govern various domains of the created order and have been subjected to Christ through his resurrection and exaltation (1:21; 3:10; 6:12). The church's existence as the multiethnic body of Christ is a public demonstration to these powers that Christ's victory has been accomplished (3:10). The spiritual warfare of 6:10-20 is not about individual moral struggle but about maintaining the church's identity as the community of the new creation against the constant pressure of the powers that resist God's purposes.

themes_detail.title: The Household Codes and the Gospel's Social Vision
themes_detail.text: Ephesians 5:22-6:9 (wives/husbands, children/parents, slaves/masters) has been the subject of intense controversy, but its theological structure is clear: every relationship is governed by 'in the Lord' and by the pattern of Christ's self-giving love. The husband's headship is modelled on Christ's self-emptying sacrifice (5:25-27), not on Roman paterfamilias authority. The code both accepts the basic structure of the Greco-Roman household (it does not call for immediate revolution) and profoundly subverts it: every superior is called to serve rather than dominate, every relationship is transformed by the gospel's pattern of mutual submission (5:21).
